figures.caption: Table 14: Empirical sketch error at k=64 (4ε = 0.0625). Events: split decisions recorded (5 seeds, ×5). Ratio: 4ε / Gini err max (worst-case safety margin). Letter is excluded; see note below.
Table 17: Accuracy comparison on standard Online CIL benchmarks (mean ± std over 5 seeds). K: number of classes; d: feature dimension.

Figure 2: Tree-dynamics diagnostics on Synth-50, Covertype, and Split-MNIST (standard, unweighted Gini criterion for all models). Top: number of leaves and cumulative splits over the stream. Bottom: weighted leaf Gini impurity and mean local class cardinality Klocal. Removing the union bound over attributes (GAINTESTNOUNION) causes systematic over-splitting—up to 6×more leaves than MIST—without a commensurate gain in leaf purity (bottom-left), confirming that the union bound is essential for controlling split proliferation as Klocal grows. VFDT (Hoeffding bound, no inheritance) shows a comparable split rate to GAINTEST but higher residual impurity, reflecting the absence of knowledge inheritance. The staircase pattern in Klocal (bottom-right) marks task boundaries; MIST’s tight McDiarmid radius delays new splits until sufficient evidence accumulates across all Klocal classes.
Table 6: Default vs. no-reset diagnostics on selected datasets (final mean accuracy, mean over seeds).

Figure 3: 2D PCA visualisations of the eight stress-test streams (50 samples per class shown). Each panel illustrates the geometric property that motivates the dataset: non-unimodal class structure (Multimodal, Skewed), manifold geometry (AngularSectors, Ring), cluster arrangement (Antipodal), and distributional properties (HeavyTail, NoisyFeature, ConceptDrift).

Table 12: Memory footprint (KB, mean over 3 seeds) and throughput (samples/s) on two representative datasets: Covertype (K = 6, d = 54, low-K regime) and Split-MNIST (K = 10, d = 50 after PCA, higher-K regime). Pareto-optimal configurations on Covertype are marked with ⋆: NCM (fastest/lightest), SLDA (fast with high accuracy), and MIST-K (highest accuracy). EFDT (D) and HAT (D) — where (D) denotes the default drift-reset configuration — are included for completeness; their near-random accuracy reflects protocol misalignment (Appendix C.2), not resource efficiency.
Table 3: Core and split-criterion ablations (average accuracy over ten standard datasets, mean over 5 seeds).

Figure 1: Effect of knowledge inheritance at the Task 3 split event on HAR. (A) New-task (Task 3) accuracy versus samples after the split: inheritance-enabled variants (MIST-G, MIST-K) remain stable while the w/o Inheritance ablation degrades to 0.00 (∆=+0.75). (B) Old-task (Task 1) accuracy over the full split window: the ablation collapses to 0.00 after the split while MIST-G retains 0.73 accuracy (∆=+0.73).
Table 7: Average forgetting (mean± std over 5 seeds) on the standard Online CIL benchmarks (lower is better). Bold: best mean within group.
